metadata.name: ALZ Additional Graph Queries
metadata.description: Azure Resource Graph queries for ALZ checklist items not covered by the original checklist

queries.text: Define and document a process to periodically audit the agreement billing RBAC role assignments to review who has access to your MCA billing account.

queries.text: Enforce a RBAC model that aligns to your cloud operating model. Scope and Assign across Management Groups and Subscriptions.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Only use the authentication type Work or school account for all account types. Avoid using the Microsoft account (MSA) account type.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Enforce Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access policies for any user with rights to Azure environments.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Enforce multi-factor authentication for any user with rights to the Azure environments.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Enforce centralized and delegated responsibilities to manage resources deployed inside the landing zone, based on role and security requirements.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Enforce Microsoft Entra ID Privileged Identity Management (PIM) to establish zero standing access and least privilege.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Microsoft recommends that you use roles with the fewest permissions. Global Administrator is a highly privileged role that should be limited to emergency scenarios when you can't use an existing role.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Deploy your Azure landing zone identity resources in multiple regions. If using domain controllers, associate each region with an Active Directory site so that resources can resolve to their local domain controllers.
queries.graph: ResourceContainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions' | extend mgChain = tolower(tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain)) | where mgChain has 'identity' | join kind=leftouter (Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.compute/virtualmachines', 'microsoft.aad/domainservices') | summarize regionCount = dcount(location) by subscriptionId) on subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(coalesce(toint(regionCount), 0) > 1, 1, 0) | project name, subscriptionId, regionCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks identity subscriptions for VM or Domain Services resources across more than one region. Multi-region deployment ensures local domain controller availability for all landing zones.

queries.text: If planning to switch from Active Directory Domain Services to Entra domain services, evaluate the compatibility of all workloads.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Integrate Microsoft Entra ID logs with the platform-central Azure Monitor. Azure Monitor allows for a single source of truth around log and monitoring data in Azure, giving organizations a cloud native options to meet requirements around log collection and retention.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Implement an emergency access or break-glass accounts to prevent tenant-wide account lockout. MFA will be turned on by default for all users in Oct 2024. We recommend updating these accounts to use passkey (FIDO2) or configure certificate-based authentication for MFA.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: When deploying Microsoft Entra Connect, use a staging sever for high availability/disaster recovery.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Do not use on-premises synced accounts for Microsoft Entra ID role assignments, unless you have a scenario that specifically requires it.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: When using Microsoft Entra ID Application Proxy to give remote users access to applications, manage it as a Platform resource.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Use Azure RBAC to manage data plane access to resources, if possible. E.g. Data Operations across Key Vault, Storage Account and Database Services.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Use Microsoft Entra ID PIM access reviews to periodically validate resource entitlements.
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Identify and restrict platform administrators in line with sovereignty requirements (for example, EU‑only, UK‑only, or country‑specific privileged roles).
queries.reason: This identity, Entra ID, PIM, MFA, or organizational access-governance requirement is not directly exposed through Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Use a well defined naming scheme for resources, such as Microsoft Best Practice Naming Standards.
queries.reason: Naming standards are organization-specific and cannot be deterministically validated from Azure Resource Graph alone.

queries.text: Enforce a sandbox management group to allow users to immediately experiment with Azure.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type == 'microsoft.management/managementgroups' | extend displayName = coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name), displayNameLower = tolower(displayName) | extend compliant = iff(displayNameLower has 'sandbox', 1, 0) | project name = displayName, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether a sandbox management group exists by looking for management groups with 'sandbox' in the display name.

queries.text: Enforce a platform management group under the root management group to support common platform policy and Azure role assignment.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type == 'microsoft.management/managementgroups' | extend displayName = coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name), displayNameLower = tolower(displayName) | extend compliant = iff(displayNameLower has 'platform', 1, 0) | project name = displayName, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether a platform management group exists by looking for management groups with 'platform' in the display name.

queries.text: Enforce a dedicated connectivity subscription in the Connectivity management group to host an Azure Virtual WAN hub, private non-AD Domain Name System (DNS), ExpressRoute circuit, and other networking resources.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions' | extend subscriptionId = name, mgmtChainText = tolower(tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain)) | where mgmtChainText has 'connectivity' | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/virtualwans','microsoft.network/virtualhubs','microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits','microsoft.network/privatednszones','microsoft.network/dnsresolvers') | summarize resourceCount = count() by subscriptionId) on subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(toint(coalesce(resourceCount, 0)) > 0, 1, 0) | project name, subscriptionId, resourceCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks subscriptions under a connectivity management-group path and whether they contain core connectivity resources such as Virtual WAN, ExpressRoute, Private DNS, or DNS Private Resolver.

queries.text: Enforce that only privileged users can operate management groups in the tenant by enabling Azure RBAC authorization in the management group hierarchy settings.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Enforce management groups under the root-level management group to represent the types of workloads, based on their security, compliance, connectivity, and feature needs.
queries.graph: ResourceContainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.management/managementgroups' | extend mgName = tolower(coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name)) | extend isWorkloadGroup = iff(mgName has_any ('corp', 'online', 'sandbox', 'platform', 'connectivity', 'identity', 'management', 'decommissioned', 'landing'), 1, 0) | extend compliant = isWorkloadGroup | project name, mgName, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks management group display names for ALZ workload-type keywords (corp, online, sandbox, platform, connectivity, identity, management, decommissioned, landing). Matches confirm the recommended management group hierarchy for workload segmentation is in place.

queries.text: Enforce a process to make resource owners aware of their roles and responsibilities, access review, budget review, policy compliance and remediate when necessary.
queries.reason: Billing or cost-management controls are outside Azure Resource Graph coverage.

queries.text: Ensure that all subscription owners and IT core team are aware of subscription quotas and the impact they have on provision resources for a given subscription.
queries.reason: This is primarily a planning, documentation, or operational-process requirement rather than a directly queryable Azure resource configuration.

queries.text: Use Reserved Instances where appropriate to optimize cost and ensure available capacity in target regions.
queries.reason: Billing or cost-management controls are outside Azure Resource Graph coverage.

queries.text: Establish dashboards and/or visualizations to monitor compute and storage capacity metrics. (i.e. CPU, memory, disk space)
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.portal/dashboards', 'microsoft.insights/workbooks') | extend compliant = 1 | project name, type, id, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Portal dashboards and Azure Monitor Workbooks, the primary tools for capacity and monitoring visualisations. Their presence indicates dashboards have been configured for compute and storage metrics.

queries.text: As part of your cloud adoption, implement a detailed cost management plan using the 'Managed cloud costs' process.
queries.reason: Billing or cost-management controls are outside Azure Resource Graph coverage.

queries.text: If servers will be used for Identity services, like domain controllers, establish a dedicated identity subscription in the identity management group, to host these services. Make sure that resources are set to use the domain controllers available in their region.
queries.graph: ResourceContainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions' | extend mgChain = tolower(tostring(properties.managementGroupAncestorsChain)) | where mgChain has 'identity' | join kind=leftouter (Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.compute/virtualmachines', 'microsoft.aad/domainservices', 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachinescalesets') | summarize idResourceCount = count() by subscriptionId) on subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(coalesce(toint(idResourceCount), 0) > 0, 1, 0) | project name, subscriptionId, idResourceCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks subscriptions in the identity management group for identity-related resources (VMs, Domain Services, VMSSs). A dedicated identity subscription should contain domain controllers or equivalent identity workloads.

queries.text: For Sovereign Landing Zone, have a 'confidential corp' and 'confidential online' management group directly under the 'landing zones' MG.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.management/managementgroups' | extend parentName = tolower(coalesce(tostring(properties.details.parent.displayName), '')) | extend mgName = tolower(coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name)) | where parentName has 'landing' | extend compliant = iff(mgName has 'confidential', 1, 0) | project name, mgName, parentName, compliant
queries.description: Checks that management groups with 'confidential' in their name exist directly under a 'landing zones' parent MG, as required for Sovereign Landing Zone deployments.

queries.text: Select the right Azure region/s for your deployment. Azure is a global-scale cloud platform that provide global coverage through many regions and geographies. Different Azure regions have different characteristics, access and availability models, costs, capacity, and services offered, then it is important to consider all criteria and requirements.
queries.reason: Region availability, capacity, or service offering validation requires external service catalog context beyond Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: Deploy your Azure landing zone in a multi-region deployment. Depending on customer size, locations, and users presence, operating in multiple regions can be a common choice to deliver services and run applications closer to them. Using a multi-region deployment is also important to provide geo disaster recovery capabilities, to eliminate the dependency from a single region capacity and diminish the risk of a temporary and localized resource capacity constraint.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions' | join kind=leftouter (resources | where isnotempty(location) | summarize regionCount=dcount(location) by subscriptionId) on subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(coalesce(toint(regionCount), 0) > 1, 1, 0) | project name, subscriptionId, regionCount=coalesce(toint(regionCount), 0), compliant
queries.description: Checks each subscription for resources deployed across more than one Azure region, indicating a multi-region landing zone deployment.

queries.text: Ensure required services and features are available within the chosen deployment regions.
queries.reason: Region availability, capacity, or service offering validation requires external service catalog context beyond Azure Resource Graph.

queries.text: For Sovereign Landing Zone, use the management group structure for Public, Confidential Corp, and Confidential Online.
queries.graph: resourcecontainers | where type == 'microsoft.management/managementgroups' | extend displayName = tolower(coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName in ('public','confidential corp','confidential online') or displayName has 'confidential corp' or displayName has 'confidential online', 1, 0) | project name = coalesce(tostring(properties.displayName), name), id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for sovereign landing zone management groups named Public, Confidential Corp, or Confidential Online.

queries.text: Define data classification levels used in your organization.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Use tags to capture sovereign workload metadata such as data classification.
queries.graph: resources | extend dataClassification = coalesce(tostring(tags['DataClassification']), tostring(tags['dataClassification']), tostring(tags['Classification']), tostring(tags['classification'])) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(dataClassification), 1, 0) | project name, id, dataClassification, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether resources carry a data-classification style tag as a proxy for sovereign workload metadata tagging.

queries.text: Document a standard for securing the delivery application content from your Workload spokes using Application Gateway and Azure Front Door. You can use the Application Delivery checklist to for recommendations.
queries.reason: This is primarily a planning, documentation, or operational-process requirement rather than a directly queryable Azure resource configuration.

queries.text: Use a hub-and-spoke network topology for network scenarios that require maximum flexibility.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks/virtualnetworkpeerings' | extend remoteVnetId = tostring(properties.remoteVirtualNetwork.id), peeringName = name | extend compliant = iff(tolower(peeringName) has 'hub' or tolower(remoteVnetId) has 'hub', 1, 0) | project name, id, remoteVnetId, compliant
queries.description: Checks VNet peering resources for hub-and-spoke naming patterns as a proxy for hub-and-spoke topology.

queries.text: Deploy your Azure landing zone connectivity resources in multiple regions, so that you can quickly support multi-region application landing zones and disaster recovery scenarios.
queries.graph: resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/virtualwans','microsoft.network/virtualhubs','microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways','microsoft.network/azurefirewalls','microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits') | summarize regionCount = dcount(location), regions = make_set(location) | extend compliant = iff(regionCount > 1, 1, 0) | project regionCount, regions, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether core connectivity resources are deployed in more than one Azure region.

queries.text: Deploy shared networking services, including ExpressRoute gateways, VPN gateways, and Azure Firewall or partner NVAs in the central-hub virtual network. If necessary, also deploy DNS services.
queries.graph: resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways','microsoft.network/azurefirewalls','microsoft.network/dnsresolvers','microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits','microsoft.network/privatednszones') | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, type, location, compliant
queries.description: Lists shared networking resources such as gateways, Azure Firewall, DNS, and ExpressRoute circuits that should exist in central networking deployments.

queries.text: When deploying partner networking technologies or NVAs, follow the partner vendor's guidance.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: If you need transit between ExpressRoute and VPN gateways in hub and spoke scenarios, use Azure Route Server.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/routeservers' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Route Server deployments that can provide ExpressRoute-to-VPN transit when required.

queries.text: For network architectures with multiple hub-and-spoke topologies across Azure regions, use global virtual network peerings between the hub VNets to connect the regions to each other.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks/virtualnetworkpeerings' | extend remoteVnetId = tostring(properties.remoteVirtualNetwork.id), localVnetName = tostring(split(id, '/')[8]), remoteVnetName = tostring(split(remoteVnetId, '/')[8]), localVnetId = substring(id, 0, indexof(id, '/virtualNetworkPeerings/')) | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project localVnetId = id, localLocation = location) on localVnetId | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project remoteVnetId = id, remoteLocation = location) on remoteVnetId | where tolower(localVnetName) has 'hub' and tolower(remoteVnetName) has 'hub' | extend compliant = iff(localLocation != remoteLocation, 1, 0) | project name, id, localLocation, remoteLocation, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether hub VNets in different regions are connected to each other through global VNet peerings.

queries.text: Use Azure Monitor for Networks to monitor the end-to-end state of the networks on Azure.
queries.graph: resources | summarize networkWatchers = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/networkwatchers'), connectionMonitors = countif(type in~ ('microsoft.network/networkwatchers/connectionmonitors','microsoft.network/connectionmonitors')) | extend compliant = iff(networkWatchers > 0 and connectionMonitors > 0, 1, 0) | project networkWatchers, connectionMonitors, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Network Watcher and Connection Monitor resources as a proxy for Azure Monitor for Networks adoption.

queries.text: Is there a requirement to encrypt traffic over the ExpressRoute?
queries.reason: This is a business or regulatory requirement question rather than a resource-state control that Azure Resource Graph can validate directly.

queries.text: When you're using ExpressRoute Direct, configure MACsec in order to encrypt traffic at the layer-two level between the organization's routers and MSEE. The diagram shows this encryption in flow.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressrouteports' | extend macsecCipher = tostring(properties.macsecConfig.cipherSuites) | extend compliant = iff(macsecCipher in ('GcmAes128', 'GcmAes256', 'GcmAesXpn128', 'GcmAesXpn256'), 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, macsecCipher, compliant
queries.description: Checks ExpressRoute Direct ports for MACsec configuration. A configured cipher suite (GcmAes128/256 or XPN variants) indicates MACsec is enabled for layer-2 encryption.

queries.text: For scenarios where MACsec isn't an option (for example, not using ExpressRoute Direct), use a VPN gateway to establish IPsec tunnels over ExpressRoute private peering.
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits', 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways') | extend rType = iff(type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits', 'ER', iff(tostring(properties.gatewayType) =~ 'Vpn', 'VPN', 'other')) | where rType in ('ER', 'VPN') | summarize hasER = countif(rType == 'ER'), hasVPN = countif(rType == 'VPN') by subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(hasER > 0 and hasVPN > 0, 1, iff(hasER == 0, 1, 0)) | project subscriptionId, hasER, hasVPN, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether subscriptions with ExpressRoute circuits also have VPN gateways deployed for IPsec tunnels over ER private peering as a MACsec alternative.

queries.text: Ensure no overlapping IP address spaces across Azure regions and on-premises locations are used.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | extend addressPrefixes = properties.addressSpace.addressPrefixes | mv-expand addressPrefix = addressPrefixes | extend prefix = tostring(addressPrefix) | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, prefix, compliant
queries.description: Lists all virtual network address prefixes across subscriptions. All rows return compliant=1; a review of the prefix list is needed to confirm no overlapping ranges exist between regions or production and DR sites.

queries.text: Do not use overlapping IP address ranges for production and disaster recovery sites.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: For environments where name resolution in Azure is all that's required, use Azure Private DNS for resolution with a delegated zone for name resolution (such as 'azure.contoso.com').
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/privatednszones' | extend compliant = iff(indexof(name, '.') > 0, 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Private DNS zones, including delegated-style zone names such as azure.contoso.com.

queries.text: For environments where name resolution across Azure and on-premises is required and there is no existing enterprise DNS service like Active Directory, use Azure DNS Private Resolver to route DNS requests to Azure or to on-premises DNS servers.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/dnsresolvers' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure DNS Private Resolver deployments.

queries.text: Special workloads that require and deploy their own DNS (such as Red Hat OpenShift) should use their preferred DNS solution.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Enable auto-registration for Azure DNS to automatically manage the lifecycle of the DNS records for the virtual machines deployed within a virtual network.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/privatednszones/virtualnetworklinks' | extend registrationEnabled = properties.registrationEnabled | extend compliant = iff(registrationEnabled == true, 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Private DNS virtual network links have auto-registration enabled.

queries.text: Implement a plan for managing DNS resolution between multiple Azure regions and when services fail over to another region
queries.reason: This is primarily a planning, documentation, or operational-process requirement rather than a directly queryable Azure resource configuration.

queries.text: Disable Windows systems from attempting to use port 65330 in Azure, Windows systems in Azure may attempt to use port 65330 for DNS resolution, which can cause timeouts and connectivity issues. Disabling this behavior can help prevent these issues.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Use Azure Bastion to securely connect to your network.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/bastionhosts' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Bastion hosts used for secure administrative access.

queries.text: Use Azure Front Door and WAF policies to provide global protection across Azure regions for inbound HTTP/S connections to a landing zone.
queries.graph: resources | summarize frontDoors = countif(type == 'microsoft.cdn/profiles' and tostring(sku.name) has 'FrontDoor'), wafPolicies = countif(type in~ ('microsoft.cdn/cdnwebapplicationfirewallpolicies','microsoft.network/frontdoorwebapplicationfirewallpolicies')) | extend compliant = iff(frontDoors > 0 and wafPolicies > 0, 1, 0) | project frontDoors, wafPolicies, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Front Door profiles together with associated WAF policy resources.

queries.text: When using Azure Front Door and Azure Application Gateway to help protect HTTP/S apps, use WAF policies in Azure Front Door. Lock down Azure Application Gateway to receive traffic only from Azure Front Door.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/frontdoors' | mv-expand ep = properties.frontendEndpoints | extend wafPolicyId = tostring(ep.properties.webApplicationFirewallPolicyLink.id) | summarize total=count(), withWaf=countif(isnotempty(wafPolicyId)) by id, name | extend compliant = iff(total == withWaf and total > 0, 1, 0) | project name, id, total, withWaf, compliant
queries.description: Checks Classic Azure Front Door resources to confirm every frontend endpoint has a WAF policy link configured.

queries.text: When WAFs and other reverse proxies are required for inbound HTTP/S connections, deploy them within a landing-zone virtual network and together with the apps that they're protecting and exposing to the internet.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/applicationgateways' | extend skuTier = tolower(tostring(properties.sku.tier)) | extend wafEnabled = tostring(properties.webApplicationFirewallConfiguration.enabled) | extend wafMode = tolower(tostring(properties.webApplicationFirewallConfiguration.firewallMode)) | extend compliant = iff((skuTier has 'waf') and wafEnabled == 'true' and wafMode == 'prevention', 1, 0) | project name, id, skuTier, wafEnabled, wafMode, compliant
queries.description: Checks Application Gateways to confirm they use a WAF_v2 tier with WAF enabled in Prevention mode for HTTP/S workload protection.

queries.text: Use Azure DDoS Network or IP Protection plans to help protect Public IP Addresses endpoints within the virtual networks including application landing zones.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | extend ddosPlanId = tostring(properties.ddosProtectionPlan.id), ddosEnabled = properties.enableDdosProtection | extend compliant = iff(ddosEnabled == true or isnotempty(ddosPlanId), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether virtual networks have Azure DDoS protection enabled or associated with a DDoS protection plan.

queries.text: Plan for how to manage your network outbound traffic configuration and strategy before the upcoming breaking change. On September 30, 2025, default outbound access for new deployments will be retired and only explicit access configurations will be allowed.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | mv-expand subnet = properties.subnets | where tostring(subnet.name) !in ('GatewaySubnet','AzureFirewallSubnet','AzureFirewallManagementSubnet','AzureBastionSubnet','RouteServerSubnet') | extend hasNatGateway = isnotempty(tostring(subnet.properties.natGateway.id)) | extend compliant = iff(hasNatGateway, 1, 0) | project vnetName=name, subnetName=tostring(subnet.name), id, compliant
queries.description: Checks each non-infrastructure VNet subnet for an explicit NAT gateway to satisfy the September 2025 default-outbound-access retirement requirement.

queries.text: Add diagnostic settings to save DDoS related logs for all the protected public IP addresses (DDoS IP or Network Protection).
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' | extend lowerId = tolower(id) | where lowerId has '/microsoft.network/publicipaddresses/' or lowerId has '/microsoft.network/ddosprotectionplans/' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for diagnostic settings attached to public IP addresses or DDoS protection plans so DDoS-related logs can be collected.

queries.text: Ensure there is a policy assignment to deny Public IP addresses directly tied to Virtual Machines. Use exclusions if public IPs are needed on specific VMs.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'public ip' or definitionId has 'publicip', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for policy assignments intended to deny or control public IP addresses on virtual machines.

queries.text: Use ExpressRoute as the primary connection to Azure. Use VPNs as a source of backup connectivity.
queries.graph: resources | summarize expressRouteCircuits = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits'), vpnGateways = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways' and tolower(tostring(properties.gatewayType)) == 'vpn') | extend compliant = iff(expressRouteCircuits > 0 and vpnGateways > 0, 1, 0) | project expressRouteCircuits, vpnGateways, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether ExpressRoute circuits and VPN gateways coexist so VPN can be used as backup connectivity.

queries.text: When you use multiple ExpressRoute circuits or multiple on-prem locations, use BGP attributes to optimize routing.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | summarize circuitCount = count(), circuits = make_list(name) by subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(circuitCount > 1, 1, 0) | project subscriptionId, circuitCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether subscriptions have more than one ExpressRoute circuit. Multiple circuits are a precondition for meaningful BGP attribute routing optimisation across on-premises locations.

queries.text: For scenarios that require bandwidth higher than 10 Gbps or dedicated 10/100-Gbps ports, use ExpressRoute Direct.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressrouteports' | extend bandwidthGbps = toint(properties.bandwidthInGbps) | extend compliant = iff(bandwidthGbps >= 10, 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, bandwidthGbps, compliant
queries.description: Checks ExpressRoute Direct port resources for bandwidth of 10 Gbps or higher. ExpressRoute Direct provides dedicated 10/100 Gbps ports directly into the Microsoft network for high-bandwidth scenarios.

queries.text: When low latency is required, or throughput from on-premises to Azure must be greater than 10 Gbps, enable FastPath to bypass the ExpressRoute gateway from the data path.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/connections' | extend connectionType = tostring(properties.connectionType), fastPath = properties.expressRouteGatewayBypass | where connectionType == 'ExpressRoute' | extend compliant = iff(fastPath == true, 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks ExpressRoute connection resources for FastPath (ExpressRoute gateway bypass) enablement.

queries.text: Use redundant VPN appliances on-premises (active/active or active/passive).
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways' | where tostring(properties.gatewayType) =~ 'Vpn' | extend activeActive = tobool(properties.activeActive) | extend compliant = iff(activeActive == true, 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, activeActive, compliant
queries.description: Checks the ARG-visible Azure side of the VPN redundancy requirement: whether Azure VPN Gateways are configured in active-active mode. Active-active gateways present two Azure IPs and maintain two BGP sessions, complementing redundant on-premises VPN appliances.

queries.text: If using ExpressRoute Direct, consider using ExpressRoute Local circuits to the local Azure regions to save costs.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend tier = tostring(sku.tier) | extend compliant = iff(tier == 'Local', 1, 0) | project name, id, tier, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether ExpressRoute circuits are using the Local tier.

queries.text: When traffic isolation or dedicated bandwidth is required, such as for separating production and nonproduction environments, use different ExpressRoute circuits. It will help you ensure isolated routing domains and alleviate noisy-neighbor risks.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend circuitSku = tostring(sku.tier) | summarize circuitCount = count(), skus = make_set(circuitSku) by subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(circuitCount > 1, 1, 0) | project subscriptionId, circuitCount, skus, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether subscriptions have more than one ExpressRoute circuit. Multiple circuits enable traffic isolation between environments (production vs non-production) and eliminate noisy-neighbour risks.

queries.text: Monitor ExpressRoute availability and utilization using built-in Express Route Insights.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend ercId = tolower(id) | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' | extend parentId = tolower(tostring(split(id, '/providers/microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings')[0])) | project parentId, diagName=name) on $left.ercId == $right.parentId | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(diagName), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks each ExpressRoute circuit for at least one diagnostic setting, enabling Express Route Insights for availability and utilization monitoring.

queries.text: Use Connection Monitor for connectivity monitoring across the network, especially between on-premises and Azure.
queries.graph: resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/networkwatchers/connectionmonitors','microsoft.network/connectionmonitors') | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, type, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Connection Monitor resources used for hybrid connectivity monitoring.

queries.text: Use site-to-site VPN as failover of ExpressRoute, if only using a single ExpressRoute circuit.
queries.graph: resources | summarize expressRouteCircuits = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits'), vpnGateways = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways' and tolower(tostring(properties.gatewayType)) == 'vpn') | extend compliant = iff(expressRouteCircuits != 1 or vpnGateways > 0, 1, 0) | project expressRouteCircuits, vpnGateways, compliant
queries.description: Checks that environments with a single ExpressRoute circuit also have a VPN gateway available for failover.

queries.text: If using ExpressRoute, your on-premises routing should be dynamic: in the event of a connection failure it should converge to the remaining connection of the circuit. Load should be shared across both connections ideally as active/active, although active/passive is supported too.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend peerings = properties.peerings | extend peeringCount = array_length(peerings) | extend hasEnabledPeering = iff(peeringCount > 0 and tostring(peerings[0].properties.state) =~ 'Enabled', 1, 0) | extend compliant = hasEnabledPeering | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, peeringCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks ExpressRoute circuits for at least one enabled BGP peering session, confirming dynamic routing is active for resilient failover.

queries.text: Ensure the two physical links of your ExpressRoute circuit are connected to two distinct edge devices in your network.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend serviceProviderProvisioningState = tostring(properties.serviceProviderProvisioningState) | extend circuitProvisioningState = tostring(properties.circuitProvisioningState) | extend compliant = iff(serviceProviderProvisioningState =~ 'Provisioned' and circuitProvisioningState =~ 'Enabled', 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, serviceProviderProvisioningState, circuitProvisioningState, compliant
queries.description: Checks ExpressRoute circuits for full service-provider provisioning and circuit enablement, confirming both physical paths are active for redundancy.

queries.text: Ensure Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) is enabled and configured on customer or provider edge routing devices.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend peerings = properties.peerings | mv-expand peering = peerings | extend peeringType = tostring(peering.properties.peeringType) | extend peeringState = tostring(peering.properties.state) | where peeringType =~ 'AzurePrivatePeering' | extend compliant = iff(peeringState =~ 'Enabled', 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, peeringType, peeringState, compliant
queries.description: Checks the ARG-visible precondition for BFD: whether ExpressRoute circuits have an enabled Azure Private Peering session. BFD requires an active BGP session; customer and provider edge BFD configuration is not visible in ARG.

queries.text: Connect the ExpressRoute Gateway to two or more circuits from different peering locations for higher resiliency.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits' | extend peeringLocation = tostring(properties.serviceProviderProperties.peeringLocation) | summarize circuitCount = count(), peeringLocations = dcount(peeringLocation), locations = make_set(peeringLocation) | extend compliant = iff(circuitCount >= 2 and peeringLocations >= 2, 1, 0) | project circuitCount, peeringLocations, locations, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether two or more ExpressRoute circuits exist and whether they use different peering locations.

queries.text: Configure diagnostic logs and alerts for ExpressRoute virtual network gateway.
queries.graph: resources | summarize diagnosticSettings = countif(type == 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' and tolower(id) has '/microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways/'), alerts = countif(type == 'microsoft.insights/metricalerts' and tolower(tostring(properties.scopes)) has 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkgateways') | extend compliant = iff(diagnosticSettings > 0 or alerts > 0, 1, 0) | project diagnosticSettings, alerts, compliant
queries.description: Checks for diagnostic settings or metric alerts scoped to ExpressRoute virtual network gateways.

queries.text: Do not use ExpressRoute circuits for VNet-to-VNet communication.
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/connections', 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkpeerings') | extend rType = iff(type =~ 'microsoft.network/connections' and tostring(properties.connectionType) =~ 'ExpressRoute', 'ER', iff(type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworkpeerings', 'PEERING', 'other')) | where rType in ('ER', 'PEERING') | summarize hasER = countif(rType == 'ER'), hasPeering = countif(rType == 'PEERING') by subscriptionId | extend compliant = iff(hasER > 0 and hasPeering > 0, 1, iff(hasER == 0, 1, 0)) | project subscriptionId, hasER, hasPeering, compliant
queries.description: Checks subscriptions with ExpressRoute connections for VNet peerings. VNet peerings enable direct VNet-to-VNet traffic over the Microsoft backbone; their absence in ER-connected subscriptions suggests VNet-to-VNet traffic may traverse the ExpressRoute circuit.

queries.text: Do not send Azure traffic to hybrid locations for inspection. Instead, follow the principle 'traffic in Azure stays in Azure' so that communication across resources in Azure occurs via the Microsoft backbone network.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/routetables' | extend routes = properties.routes | mv-expand route = routes | extend addressPrefix = tostring(route.properties.addressPrefix) | extend nextHopType = tostring(route.properties.nextHopType) | where addressPrefix == '0.0.0.0/0' | extend compliant = iff(nextHopType in~ ('VirtualAppliance', 'Internet', 'None'), 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, nextHopType, compliant
queries.description: Checks user-defined routes for default route (0.0.0.0/0) next-hop type. Routes pointing to VirtualAppliance (Azure Firewall or NVA) keep traffic in Azure; routes pointing to VirtualNetworkGateway may forward traffic to on-premises, violating the principle that Azure traffic stays in Azure.

queries.text: Do any interconnects (ExpressRoute, MPLS etc) traverse borders or different countries?
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Ensure VNet peering's do not enable traffic to leave approved regions.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks/virtualnetworkpeerings' | extend remoteVnetId = tostring(properties.remoteVirtualNetwork.id), localVnetId = substring(id, 0, indexof(id, '/virtualNetworkPeerings/')) | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project localVnetId = id, localLocation = location) on localVnetId | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project remoteVnetId = id, remoteLocation = location) on remoteVnetId | extend compliant = iff(localLocation == remoteLocation, 1, 0) | project name, id, localLocation, remoteLocation, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether peered VNets stay within the same region as a proxy for approved-region peering rules.

queries.text: Use Azure Firewall to govern Azure outbound traffic to the internet, non-HTTP/S inbound connections, and East/West traffic filtering (if the organization requires it).
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Firewall deployments.

queries.text: Create a global Azure Firewall policy to govern security posture across the global network environment and assign it to all Azure Firewall instances. Allow for granular policies to meet requirements of specific regions by delegating incremental firewall policies to local security teams via Azure role-based access control.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls' | extend firewallPolicyId = tostring(properties.firewallPolicy.id) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(firewallPolicyId), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether each Azure Firewall is associated with a Firewall Policy.

queries.text: Configure supported partner SaaS security providers within Firewall Manager if the organization wants to use such solutions to help protect outbound connections.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/securitypartnerproviders' | extend partnerName = tostring(properties.securityProviderName) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(partnerName), 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, partnerName, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Firewall Manager security partner provider resources, which enable third-party SaaS security filtering of outbound internet connections via a Virtual WAN hub.

queries.text: Add diagnostic settings to save logs, using the Resource Specific destination table, for all Azure Firewall deployments.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' | extend lowerId = tolower(id), destinationType = tostring(properties.logAnalyticsDestinationType) | where lowerId has '/microsoft.network/azurefirewalls/' | extend compliant = iff(destinationType == 'Dedicated', 1, 0) | project name, id, destinationType, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Azure Firewall diagnostic settings use the resource-specific Log Analytics destination table (Dedicated).

queries.text: Migrate from Azure Firewall Classic rules (if exist) to Firewall Policy.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls' | extend firewallPolicyId = tostring(properties.firewallPolicy.id) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(firewallPolicyId), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Azure Firewalls are using Firewall Policy instead of classic rule configuration.

queries.text: Arrange rules within the firewall policy into Rule Collection Groups and Rule Collections and based on their frequency of use.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies/rulecollectiongroups' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Firewall Policy rule collection groups.

queries.text: Use IP Groups or IP prefixes to reduce number of IP table rules.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/ipgroups' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure IP Groups that can be reused in firewall rules.

queries.text: Do not use wildcards as a source IP for DNATS, such as * or any, you should specify source IPs for incoming DNATs.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies/rulecollectiongroups' | mv-expand ruleCollection = properties.ruleCollections | mv-expand rule = ruleCollection.rules | where tolower(tostring(rule.ruleType)) == 'natrule' | extend sourceAddresses = tostring(rule.sourceAddresses) | extend compliant = iff(sourceAddresses has '"*"' or sourceAddresses has 'Any', 0, 1) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks Firewall Policy NAT rules for wildcard or Any source addresses.

queries.text: Prevent SNAT Port exhaustion by monitoring SNAT port usage, evaluating NAT Gateway settings, and ensuring seamless failover. If the port count approaches the limit, it’s a sign that SNAT exhaustion might be imminent.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls' | extend ipConfigCount = array_length(properties.ipConfigurations) | extend compliant = iff(toint(ipConfigCount) >= 2, 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, ipConfigCount, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure Firewalls for at least two public IP configurations. Each additional IP provides ~2048 extra SNAT ports, reducing the risk of SNAT port exhaustion under high outbound load.

queries.text: If you are using Azure Firewall Premium, enable TLS Inspection.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies' | extend tier = tostring(coalesce(properties.sku.tier, sku.tier)), caName = tostring(properties.transportSecurity.certificateAuthority.name) | where tier == 'Premium' | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(caName), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Premium Firewall Policies have TLS inspection certificate authority configuration.

queries.text: Use web categories to allow or deny outbound access to specific topics.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies/rulecollectiongroups' | mv-expand ruleCollection = properties.ruleCollections | mv-expand rule = ruleCollection.rules | extend webCategories = tostring(rule.webCategories) | where isnotempty(webCategories) and webCategories != '[]' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks Firewall Policy rules for configured web categories.

queries.text: As part of your TLS inspection, plan for receiving traffic from Azure App Gateways for inspection.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies' | extend tlsCertAuthority = tostring(properties.transportSecurity.certificateAuthority.name) | extend tlsEnabled = isnotempty(tlsCertAuthority) | extend compliant = iff(tlsEnabled, 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, tlsCertAuthority, tlsEnabled, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure Firewall Policies for TLS inspection configuration. A configured certificate authority in the transport security policy indicates TLS inspection is enabled for inspecting HTTPS traffic from App Gateways and other sources.

queries.text: Implement backups for your firewall rules
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls' | extend firewallPolicy = tostring(properties.firewallPolicy.id) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(firewallPolicy), 1, 0) | project name, id, firewallPolicy, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure Firewalls for an associated Firewall Policy resource, enabling IaC-based rule management and backup of firewall configuration.

queries.text: Do not disrupt control-plane communication for Azure PaaS services injected into a virtual networks, such as with a 0.0.0.0/0 route or an NSG rule that blocks control plane traffic.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | mv-expand subnet = properties.subnets | extend hasDelegation = array_length(subnet.properties.delegations) > 0 | extend routeTableId = tolower(tostring(subnet.properties.routeTable.id)) | where hasDelegation and isnotempty(routeTableId) | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/routetables' | mv-expand route = properties.routes | where route.properties.addressPrefix == '0.0.0.0/0' and route.properties.nextHopType == 'VirtualAppliance' | project rtId=tolower(id), hasDefaultRoute=1) on $left.routeTableId == $right.rtId | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(hasDefaultRoute), 0, 1) | project vnetName=name, subnetName=tostring(subnet.name), routeTableId, compliant
queries.description: Detects subnets with PaaS service delegations that have a route table containing a 0.0.0.0/0 route to a virtual appliance, which can disrupt Azure PaaS control-plane traffic.

queries.text: Use Private Link, where available, for shared Azure PaaS services.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/privateendpoints' | extend targetResource = tostring(properties.privateLinkServiceConnections[0].properties.privateLinkServiceId) | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, targetResource, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Private Endpoint resources used to access PaaS services privately.

queries.text: Access Azure PaaS services from on-premises via private endpoints and ExpressRoute private peering. This method avoids transiting over the public internet.
queries.graph: resources | summarize privateEndpoints = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/privateendpoints'), expressRouteCircuits = countif(type == 'microsoft.network/expressroutecircuits') | extend compliant = iff(privateEndpoints > 0 and expressRouteCircuits > 0, 1, 0) | project privateEndpoints, expressRouteCircuits, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether both Private Endpoints and ExpressRoute circuits exist as a proxy for private on-premises access to PaaS services.

queries.text: Filter egress traffic to Azure PaaS services using FQDNs instead of IP addresses in Azure Firewall or an NVA to prevent data exfiltration. If using Private Link you can block all FQDNs, otherwise allow only the required PaaS services.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/firewallpolicies/rulecollectiongroups' | mv-expand ruleCollection = properties.ruleCollections | mv-expand rule = ruleCollection.rules | where tolower(tostring(rule.ruleType)) == 'applicationrule' | extend targetFqdns = tostring(rule.targetFqdns), fqdnTags = tostring(rule.fqdnTags) | extend compliant = iff((isnotempty(targetFqdns) and targetFqdns != '[]') or (isnotempty(fqdnTags) and fqdnTags != '[]'), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Azure Firewall application rules use target FQDNs or FQDN tags to filter egress traffic.

queries.text: Delegate subnet creation to the landing zone owner.
queries.reason: This requirement depends on organizational intent, operational practice, or external context that Azure Resource Graph cannot validate directly.

queries.text: Use NSGs and application security groups to micro-segment traffic within the landing zone and avoid using a central NVA to filter traffic flows.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | mv-expand subnet = properties.subnets | extend subnetName = tostring(subnet.name), nsgId = tostring(subnet.properties.networkSecurityGroup.id) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(nsgId), 1, 0) | project name, id, subnetName, nsgId, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether subnets have NSG associations as a proxy for network micro-segmentation.

queries.text: Use Virtual WAN if your scenario is explicitly described in the list of Virtual WAN routing designs.
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualhubs' | extend hubSku = tostring(properties.sku) | extend compliant = iff(hubSku in~ ('Standard', 'Basic'), 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, hubSku, compliant
queries.description: Checks Virtual WAN hubs for a defined SKU tier (Standard or Basic), confirming they are configured as part of an explicit Virtual WAN routing scenario.

queries.text: Use a Virtual WAN hub per Azure region to connect multiple landing zones together across Azure regions via a common global Azure Virtual WAN.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualhubs' | summarize hubCount = count(), regionCount = dcount(location), regions = make_set(location) | extend compliant = iff(hubCount == regionCount and hubCount > 0, 1, 0) | project hubCount, regionCount, regions, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Virtual WAN hubs exist and whether there is one hub per distinct region.

queries.text: Ensure that your virtual WAN network architecture aligns to an identified architecture scenario.
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.network/virtualwans', 'microsoft.network/virtualhubs') | extend vwanType = tostring(properties.type) | extend compliant = 1 | project name, type, id, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, vwanType, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Virtual WAN and Virtual Hub resources. Their presence confirms the network architecture uses Virtual WAN and can be aligned to an identified routing scenario.

queries.text: Use Azure Monitor Insights for Virtual WAN to monitor the end-to-end topology of the Virtual WAN, status, and key metrics.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualwans' | extend wanId = tolower(id) | join kind=leftouter (resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' | extend parentId = tolower(tostring(split(id, '/providers/microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings')[0])) | project parentId, diagName=name) on $left.wanId == $right.parentId | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(diagName), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks Virtual WAN resources for at least one diagnostic setting, enabling Azure Monitor Insights for end-to-end topology and key metrics.

queries.text: Configure label-based propagation in Virtual WAN, otherwise connectivity between virtual hubs will be impaired.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/virtualhubs/hubvirtualnetworkconnections' | extend labels = properties.routingConfiguration.propagatedRouteTables.labels | extend compliant = iff(toint(coalesce(array_length(labels), 0)) > 0, 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Virtual WAN VNet connections have propagated route-table labels configured.

queries.text: Leverage Azure Policy strategically, define controls for your environment, using Policy Initiatives to group related policies.
queries.graph: policyresources | summarize initiatives = countif(type == 'microsoft.authorization/policysetdefinitions'), initiativeAssignments = countif(type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' and tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) has 'policysetdefinitions') | extend compliant = iff(initiatives > 0 and initiativeAssignments > 0, 1, 0) | project initiatives, initiativeAssignments, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether policy initiatives exist and whether policy assignments target initiative definitions.

queries.text: Map regulatory and compliance requirements to Azure Policy definitions and Azure role assignments.
queries.graph: PolicyResources | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend scope = tostring(properties.scope) | extend isMgScope = iff(scope startswith '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/', 1, 0) | extend compliant = isMgScope | project name, id, scope, isMgScope, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure Policy assignments for management group scope. Assignments at management group level ensure regulatory and compliance requirements are enforced hierarchically across all child subscriptions.

queries.text: Establish Azure Policy definitions at the intermediate root management group so that they can be assigned at inherited scopes.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policydefinitions' | extend lowerId = tolower(id) | extend compliant = iff(lowerId has '/providers/microsoft.management/managementgroups/', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for policy definitions created at management-group scope.

queries.text: Manage policy assignments at the highest appropriate level with exclusions at bottom levels, if required.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend scope = tolower(tostring(properties.scope)) | extend compliant = iff(scope has '/providers/microsoft.management/managementgroups/', 1, 0) | project name, id, scope, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether policy assignments are being applied at management-group scope.

queries.text: Use Azure Policy to control which services users can provision at the subscription/management group level.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'allowed resource types' or displayName has 'not allowed resource types' or definitionId has 'allowedresourcetypes' or definitionId has 'notallowedresourcetypes', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for policy assignments that restrict which Azure resource types can be deployed.

queries.text: Use built-in policies where possible to minimize operational overhead.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(definitionId !has '/subscriptions/' and definitionId !has '/providers/microsoft.management/managementgroups/', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether policy assignments reference built-in policy definitions rather than subscription- or management-group-scoped custom definitions.

queries.text: Assign the built-in Resource Policy Contributor role at a particular scope to enable application-level governance.
queries.graph: authorizationresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments' | extend roleDefinitionName = tostring(properties.roleDefinitionName) | extend compliant = iff(roleDefinitionName == 'Resource Policy Contributor', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Resource Policy Contributor role assignments.

queries.text: Limit the number of Azure Policy assignments made at the root management group scope to avoid managing through exclusions at inherited scopes.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend scope = tolower(tostring(properties.scope)) | where scope has '/providers/microsoft.management/managementgroups/' | summarize assignmentCount = count() | extend compliant = iff(assignmentCount <= 10, 1, 0) | project assignmentCount, compliant
queries.description: Counts management-group-scoped policy assignments as a proxy for limiting top-level policy assignment sprawl.

queries.text: Configure 'Actual' and 'Forecasted' Budget Alerts.
queries.reason: Billing or cost-management controls are outside Azure Resource Graph coverage.

queries.text: If any data sovereignty requirements exist, Azure Policies should be deployed to enforce them.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend defId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)) | extend compliant = iff(defId contains 'e56962a6' or defId contains 'e765b65a' or displayName has 'allowed location', 1, 0) | project name, scope=tostring(properties.scope), displayName, compliant
queries.description: Checks for 'Allowed locations' policy assignments that restrict resource deployment to approved regions, enforcing data sovereignty requirements.

queries.text: For Sovereign Landing Zone, deploy the global and confidential policies and assign them at the correct management group level.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), scope = tostring(properties.scope) | where displayName has 'global' or displayName has 'confidential' | extend compliant = iff(tolower(scope) has '/providers/microsoft.management/managementgroups/', 1, 0) | project name, id, scope, compliant
queries.description: Checks for sovereign landing zone policy assignments with global or confidential naming at management-group scope.

queries.text: For Sovereign Landing Zone, document your sovereign control objectives and align them to policy.
queries.reason: This is primarily a planning, documentation, or operational-process requirement rather than a directly queryable Azure resource configuration.

queries.text: Ensure primary and DR regions are inside the allowed jurisdiction (including paired region behavior, ZRS/GZRS constraints).
queries.graph: Resources | where isnotempty(location) and location != 'global' | summarize resourceCount = count() by location | extend compliant = 1 | project location, resourceCount, compliant
queries.description: Lists all Azure regions where resources are deployed with resource counts per region. All rows return compliant=1; a review is required to verify each region is within the organisation's permitted jurisdictional boundaries for primary and DR deployments.

queries.text: For landing zone services (Databases, Storage, Containers, Analytics, AI), ensure the data‑processing locations and feature availabilities are acceptable within permitted regions or sovereignty constraints.
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.sql/servers', 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts', 'microsoft.documentdb/databaseaccounts', 'microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters', 'microsoft.cognitiveservices/accounts', 'microsoft.dbforpostgresql/flexibleservers', 'microsoft.dbformysql/flexibleservers') | extend compliant = 1 | project name, type, id, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, compliant
queries.description: Lists core landing zone PaaS services with their deployment locations. All rows return compliant=1; a review is required to verify data-processing locations are within permitted regions or sovereignty constraints.

queries.text: Have jurisdiction constraints been established for vendor remote access and managed services personnel?
queries.reason: This sovereignty requirement depends on personnel, support process, or contractual controls that Azure Resource Graph does not expose.

queries.text: Do you require Azure confidential computing for sensitive workloads?
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines' | extend securityType = tostring(properties.securityProfile.securityType) | extend isConfidential = iff(securityType =~ 'ConfidentialVM', 1, 0) | extend compliant = isConfidential | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, securityType, compliant
queries.description: Checks VMs for the ConfidentialVM security type, enabling hardware-based trusted execution environments. Use this to verify sensitive workloads have confidential computing enabled where required.

queries.text: Use a single monitor logs workspace to manage platforms centrally except where Azure role-based access control (Azure RBAC), data sovereignty requirements, or data retention policies mandate separate workspaces.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces' | summarize workspaceCount = count(), regions = make_set(location) | extend compliant = iff(workspaceCount == 1, 1, 0) | project workspaceCount, regions, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether a single Log Analytics workspace is being used centrally.

queries.text: Decide whether to use a single Azure Monitor Logs workspace for all regions or to create multiple workspaces to cover various geographical regions. Each approach has advantages and disadvantages, including potential cross-region networking charges
queries.graph: Resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces' | extend retentionDays = toint(properties.retentionInDays) | extend compliant = iff(retentionDays >= 30, 1, 0) | project name, id, location, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, retentionDays, compliant
queries.description: Lists all Log Analytics workspaces with their retention periods. Workspaces with fewer than 30 days retention are flagged non-compliant. The workspace and region distribution supports the single vs multi-workspace architecture decision.

queries.text: Export logs to Azure Storage if your log retention requirements exceed twelve years. Use immutable storage with a write-once, read-many policy to make data non-erasable and non-modifiable for a user-specified interval.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts' | extend immutableEnabled = tobool(properties.immutableStorageWithVersioning.enabled) | extend compliant = iff(immutableEnabled == true, 1, 0) | project name, id, kind=tostring(kind), compliant
queries.description: Checks storage accounts for immutable storage with versioning enabled, satisfying the write-once read-many policy required for long-term log retention beyond twelve years.

queries.text: Monitor OS level virtual machine (VM) configuration drift using Azure Policy. Enabling Azure Automanage Machine Configuration audit capabilities through policy helps application team workloads to immediately consume feature capabilities with little effort.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'machine configuration' or displayName has 'guest configuration' or definitionId has 'guestconfiguration' or definitionId has 'machineconfiguration', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Policy assignments related to Guest Configuration or Machine Configuration for VM drift monitoring.

queries.text: Use Azure Update Manager as a patching mechanism for Windows and Linux VMs in Azure.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines' | extend patchMode = coalesce(tostring(properties.osProfile.windowsConfiguration.patchSettings.patchMode), tostring(properties.osProfile.linuxConfiguration.patchSettings.patchMode)), assessmentMode = coalesce(tostring(properties.osProfile.windowsConfiguration.patchSettings.assessmentMode), tostring(properties.osProfile.linuxConfiguration.patchSettings.assessmentMode)) | extend compliant = iff(patchMode == 'AutomaticByPlatform' or assessmentMode == 'AutomaticByPlatform', 1, 0) | project name, id, patchMode, assessmentMode, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure VM patch settings for Azure Update Manager style platform-driven assessment or patch orchestration.

queries.text: Use Azure Update Manager as a patching mechanism for Windows and Linux VMs outside of Azure using Azure Arc.
queries.graph: resources | where type =~ 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines' | extend arcId = tolower(id) | join kind=leftouter (maintenanceresources | where type =~ 'microsoft.maintenance/configurationassignments' | extend parentId = tolower(tostring(split(id, '/providers/microsoft.maintenance/configurationassignments')[0])) | project parentId, assignmentName=name) on $left.arcId == $right.parentId | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(assignmentName), 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks Azure Arc-connected machines for an assigned maintenance configuration, indicating enrollment in Azure Update Manager for patching outside of Azure.

queries.text: Use Network Watcher to proactively monitor traffic flows.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.network/networkwatchers' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Network Watcher resources.

queries.text: Use resource locks to prevent accidental deletion of critical shared services.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/locks' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for management locks on Azure resources.

queries.text: Use deny policies to supplement Azure role assignments. The combination of deny policies and Azure role assignments ensures the appropriate guardrails are in place to enforce who can deploy and configure resources and what resources they can deploy and configure.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'deny' or definitionId has 'deny', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for deny-style policy assignments used as additional guardrails alongside RBAC.

queries.text: Include service and resource health events as part of the overall platform monitoring solution. Tracking service and resource health from the platform perspective is an important component of resource management in Azure.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.insights/activitylogalerts' | extend conditionText = tolower(tostring(properties.condition)) | extend compliant = iff(conditionText has 'servicehealth' or conditionText has 'resourcehealth', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for activity log alerts covering Service Health or Resource Health events.

queries.text: Include alerts and action groups as part of the Azure Service Health platform to ensure that alerts or issues can be actioned.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.insights/activitylogalerts' | extend conditionText = tolower(tostring(properties.condition)), actionGroups = properties.actions.actionGroups | where conditionText has 'servicehealth' | extend compliant = iff(toint(coalesce(array_length(actionGroups), 0)) > 0, 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks whether Service Health activity log alerts have action groups configured.

queries.text: Don't send raw log entries back to on-premises monitoring systems. Instead, adopt a principle that data born in Azure stays in Azure. If on-premises SIEM integration is required, then send critical alerts instead of logs.
queries.reason: This depends on on-premises or provider-side configuration that Azure Resource Graph cannot see.

queries.text: Use Azure Monitor Logs for insights and reporting.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces' | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, location, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Log Analytics workspaces used by Azure Monitor Logs.

queries.text: When necessary, use shared storage accounts within the landing zone for Azure diagnostic extension log storage.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.insights/diagnosticsettings' | extend storageAccountId = tostring(properties.storageAccountId) | extend compliant = iff(isnotempty(storageAccountId), 1, 0) | project name, id, storageAccountId, compliant
queries.description: Checks for diagnostic settings that send logs to Azure Storage accounts.

queries.text: Use Azure Monitor alerts for the generation of operational alerts.
queries.graph: resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.insights/metricalerts','microsoft.insights/scheduledqueryrules','microsoft.insights/activitylogalerts') | extend compliant = 1 | project name, id, type, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Monitor alert resources such as metric alerts, scheduled query rules, and activity log alerts.

queries.text: Ensure that monitoring requirements have been assessed and that appropriate data collection and alerting configurations are applied.
queries.graph: Resources | where type in~ ('microsoft.insights/metricalerts', 'microsoft.insights/scheduledqueryrules', 'microsoft.insights/activitylogalerts') | extend alertEnabled = tobool(properties.enabled) | extend compliant = iff(alertEnabled == true, 1, 0) | project name, type, id, location, resourceGroup, alertEnabled, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Monitor alert rules (metric alerts, log query rules, and activity log alerts) and whether they are enabled. The presence of enabled rules indicates monitoring and alerting requirements have been implemented.

queries.text: Deploy AMBA to establish monitoring for platform components of your landing zone - AMBA is a framework solution that is available and provides an easy way to scale alerting by using Azure Policy.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'monitor baseline alerts' or displayName has 'amba' or definitionId has 'monitor_baseline_alerts', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Azure Monitor Baseline Alerts (AMBA) policy assignments.

queries.text: Employ Azure Site Recovery for Azure-to-Azure VM DR scenarios. Use Azure-native backup capabilities.
queries.graph: resources | summarize recoveryVaults = countif(type == 'microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults'), replicationResources = countif(type has 'microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults/replication') | extend compliant = iff(recoveryVaults > 0 and replicationResources > 0, 1, 0) | project recoveryVaults, replicationResources, compliant
queries.description: Checks for Recovery Services vaults and replication-related resources as a proxy for Azure Site Recovery adoption.

queries.text: When using Azure Backup, use the correct backup types (GRS, ZRS & LRS) for your backup, as the default setting is GRS.
queries.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults' | extend redundancy = coalesce(tostring(properties.redundancySettings.standardTierStorageRedundancy), tostring(properties.backupStorageConfig.storageModelType), tostring(properties.storageModelType)) | extend compliant = iff(redundancy in~ ('GeoRedundant','ZoneRedundant','LocallyRedundant','GRS','ZRS','LRS'), 1, 0) | project name, id, redundancy, compliant
queries.description: Checks Recovery Services vault backup storage redundancy configuration.

queries.text: Use Azure guest policies to automatically deploy software configurations through VM extensions and enforce a compliant baseline VM configuration.
queries.graph: policyresources | where type == 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments' | extend displayName = tolower(tostring(properties.displayName)), definitionId = tolower(tostring(properties.policyDefinitionId)) | extend compliant = iff(displayName has 'guest configuration' or definitionId has 'guestconfiguration', 1, 0) | project name, id, compliant
queries.description: Checks for guest-configuration policy assignments that can deploy VM extensions and enforce baseline settings.

queries.text: Monitor VM security configuration drift via Azure Policy.
